question: Observable vs Promise.
answer:
• Observable is lazy (runs on subscribe); a Promise is eager.
  • Observable emits multiple values over time; a Promise resolves once.
  • Observable is cancellable (unsubscribe); a Promise isn't.
  • Observables have a rich operator pipeline (map, filter, switchMap…).
tip: Cancellability is the killer feature — it's why type-ahead search uses observables.

question: switchMap vs mergeMap vs concatMap vs exhaustMap.
answer:
• switchMap — cancels the previous inner observable when a new value arrives. Type-ahead search, cancel stale API calls.
  • mergeMap — runs all inner observables concurrently, no order guarantee. Parallel independent requests.
  • concatMap — queues them one after another in order. Ordered writes/saves.
  • exhaustMap — ignores new values while one is still running. Prevent double form submits / login spam.
tip: The single most-asked RxJS question — memorise the use-case for each.

question: Subject vs BehaviorSubject vs ReplaySubject vs AsyncSubject.
answer:
• Subject — multicast; subscribers only get values emitted after they subscribe.
  • BehaviorSubject — holds a current value, needs an initial value, emits the latest immediately on subscribe.
  • ReplaySubject — replays the last N values to new subscribers.
  • AsyncSubject — emits only the last value, and only on complete.
tip: BehaviorSubject is the go-to for shared state like the current user.

question: How do you avoid memory leaks / unsubscribe?
answer:
• async pipe in the template — subscribes and unsubscribes automatically (preferred).
  • takeUntil(destroy$) with a Subject completed in ngOnDestroy.
  • takeUntilDestroyed() in modern Angular.
  • Store the Subscription and call unsubscribe() in ngOnDestroy.

HTTP calls complete on their own, but long-lived streams (Subjects, valueChanges, intervals) leak if you don't clean up.
tip: async pipe first; takeUntil when you must subscribe in the class.

question: Hot vs cold observables.
answer:
Cold — each subscriber triggers its own producer, getting an independent execution (e.g. an HTTP call runs per subscribe).

Hot — the producer is shared; subscribers see values as they happen (e.g. DOM events, a Subject). Use share() / shareReplay() to make a cold stream hot.
tip: Wrap an HTTP call with shareReplay(1) to avoid firing it once per subscriber.

question: Name common RxJS operators and what they do.
answer:
• map / filter — transform / keep values.
  • tap — side effects (logging) without changing the stream.
  • debounceTime / distinctUntilChanged — throttle noisy input.
  • take / takeUntil — limit emissions.
  • catchError — handle errors and recover.
tip: tap is for side effects only — never mutate values inside it.

question: How do you handle errors in RxJS?
answer:
Use catchError to intercept an error and return a fallback observable (or rethrow). retry(n) / retryWhen re-subscribe on failure.

Remember: an error terminates the stream — nothing emits after it, so handle it inside the inner observable if you want the outer stream to survive.
tip: In a switchMap search, put catchError inside the inner call so one failed search doesn't kill the whole stream.

question: combineLatest vs forkJoin vs merge vs zip.
answer:
• combineLatest — emits the latest of each source whenever any emits.
  • forkJoin — waits for all to complete, emits the last value of each (like Promise.all).
  • merge — interleaves emissions from all sources.
  • zip — pairs emissions by index.
tip: forkJoin is the RxJS equivalent of Promise.all for parallel HTTP calls.
code:
forkJoin({ user: this.getUser(), roles: this.getRoles() })
  .subscribe(({ user, roles }) => {});

question: of vs from vs fromEvent.
answer:
• of(1,2,3) — emits the given values then completes.
  • from(arrayOrPromise) — turns an array/iterable/Promise into an observable.
  • fromEvent(el,'click') — an observable of DOM events.
tip: from unpacks an array element-by-element; of emits the array as a single value.
code:
of([1, 2, 3]);   // one emission: [1,2,3]
from([1, 2, 3]); // three emissions: 1, 2, 3

question: What is the pipe() method?
answer: pipe() composes operators left to right into a new observable. Pipeable operators are tree-shakable pure functions — you import only what you use.
tip: Order matters — debounceTime before switchMap, not after.

question: Why pair debounceTime with distinctUntilChanged in search?
answer: debounceTime waits for the user to pause typing; distinctUntilChanged drops emissions equal to the previous one (e.g. type then delete back to the same term) so you don't fire a duplicate request.
tip: This trio — debounceTime, distinctUntilChanged, switchMap — is the canonical search pattern.

question: What does startWith do?
answer: Emits one or more seed values before the source starts — handy for showing an initial/default UI state (e.g. a loading flag or empty list) before real data arrives.
tip: Combine startWith with an async pipe to render an initial value instantly.

question: Show the canonical type-ahead search pipeline.
answer: Combine the four operators every interviewer wants: debounceTime (wait for a pause), distinctUntilChanged (skip duplicate terms), switchMap (cancel stale requests), and catchError inside the inner call (so one failure doesn't kill the stream).
tip: Put catchError on the inner observable, not the outer — otherwise the stream dies after one error.
code:
input$.pipe(
  debounceTime(300),
  distinctUntilChanged(),
  switchMap(q => this.api.search(q).pipe(catchError(() => of([]))))
)

question: How do you poll an API every N seconds with RxJS?
answer: Use timer(0, N) to emit immediately then every N ms, and switchMap to the request (so a slow response doesn't overlap the next tick). Stop with takeUntil.
tip: switchMap prevents overlapping requests if one poll is slower than the interval.

question: How do you retry a failed request with exponential backoff?
answer: Use retry with a delay function (RxJS 7.4+) that returns an increasing delay per attempt, or retryWhen in older versions. Cap the number of retries so it doesn't loop forever.
tip: Exponential backoff avoids hammering a struggling server — mention capping the retries.

question: How do you cache an HTTP result across subscribers?
answer: Pipe the request through shareReplay(1): the underlying call runs once, and every current/future subscriber gets the last emitted value — a simple in-memory cache. Add { refCount: true } to release it when no one's subscribed.
tip: shareReplay(1) turns a per-subscriber HTTP call into a single shared, cached one.

question: What does scan do (vs reduce)?
answer: scan is like reduce but emits the accumulated value on every emission, not just at the end — perfect for accumulating state over a stream (a running total, or building up a list from actions).
tip: scan is the RxJS building block for a reducer-style state stream.

question: combineLatest vs withLatestFrom.
answer: combineLatest emits when any source emits. withLatestFrom emits only when the source emits, pulling in the latest value of the others — use it when one stream is the trigger and the rest are just context.
tip: withLatestFrom = 'when A fires, also give me the current B' — one driver, others passive.

question: What is finalize used for?
answer: finalize runs a callback when the observable completes, errors, or is unsubscribed — the RxJS equivalent of finally. Common use: hide a loading spinner regardless of success or failure.
tip: finalize is the clean place to turn off a loading flag — it always runs.

question: debounceTime vs throttleTime vs auditTime vs sampleTime.
answer:
• debounceTime — emit after a quiet gap (last value).
  • throttleTime — emit the first value, then ignore for a window.
  • auditTime — ignore for a window, then emit the latest value.
  • sampleTime — emit the latest value on a fixed interval.
tip: debounce for search, throttle for scroll/resize, sample for periodic snapshots.

question: What are higher-order observables and *All operators?
answer: A higher-order observable emits other observables. Flattening operators subscribe to the inner ones: mergeAll (concurrent), concatAll (in order), switchAll (cancel previous). The *Map variants (mergeMap, concatMap…) are just map + *All.
tip: switchMap === map(...) + switchAll — knowing this shows deep RxJS understanding.
code:
source$.pipe(map(id => this.load(id)), concatAll());
// same as: source$.pipe(concatMap(id => this.load(id)))

question: What is takeUntilDestroyed?
answer: (Angular 16+) An operator that auto-completes a subscription when the component/directive is destroyed — replacing the manual destroy$ Subject + takeUntil boilerplate. Call it in an injection context, or pass a DestroyRef.
tip: It's the modern, boilerplate-free way to prevent subscription leaks.

question: How do you write a custom operator?
answer: A pipeable operator is just a function that takes a source observable and returns a new one — usually by composing existing operators with pipe. This makes reusable, named stream transformations.
tip: A custom operator is often just a named composition of built-in operators.

question: What are RxJS schedulers?
answer: A scheduler controls when and on what context emissions happen — e.g. asyncScheduler (macrotask), asapScheduler (microtask), animationFrameScheduler (rAF). Used for fine timing control and for virtual time in tests (TestScheduler).
tip: Most code never touches schedulers directly — but marble tests rely on the TestScheduler.

question: How do you convert an Observable to a Promise?
answer: Use firstValueFrom(obs$) (resolves with the first emission) or lastValueFrom(obs$) (waits for completion). These replaced the deprecated .toPromise(). Handy when awaiting a one-shot stream in async/await code.
tip: .toPromise() is deprecated — use firstValueFrom / lastValueFrom.

question: Why does my HTTP call fire twice with two async pipes?
answer: Observables are cold — each subscription re-runs the producer, so two async pipes on the same http.get() trigger two requests. Fix by multicasting with shareReplay(1), or subscribe once and reuse the value.
tip: Two async pipes on one HTTP observable = two network calls unless you shareReplay it.

question: What happens to a stream after it errors?
answer: An error is a terminal event — the observable stops and emits nothing further, and the subscription is torn down. To keep a long-lived stream alive (like a search box), handle the error inside the inner observable with catchError so the outer stream never sees it.
tip: Error and complete both end a stream permanently — design for that.

question: How do Observables and Signals interoperate?
answer: (Angular 16+) toSignal(obs$) subscribes and exposes the latest value as a signal (auto-unsubscribes); toObservable(sig) turns a signal into a stream. Use RxJS for async/events, signals for synchronous local state, and bridge between them.
tip: toSignal replaces the async pipe in signal-based components and auto-cleans up.

question: When should you use tap?
answer: tap runs a side effect (logging, setting a flag, triggering an action) without altering the emitted value or the stream. Great for debugging and for effects that shouldn't change data. Never mutate values inside it.
tip: tap is for side effects only — if you're transforming the value, use map.
